meta.title: Occupational Health Externalities — Goguen Institution Parameter Lab
meta.subtitle: A Monte Carlo proxy simulator for a Goguen-style institution of occupational health externalities. Adjust signature-level conditions, sentence-strength parameters, model conditions, and internalisation mechanisms to explore how workplace harms and benefits are externalised or internalised across firms, households, public systems, and communities.
meta.footerNote: Note: This simulator is a conceptual proxy for exploring tensions within an institutional account of occupational health externalities. It is not an empirical estimator, legal advice, or a substitute for occupational hygiene, epidemiology, or policy evaluation.

project.name: Goguen Institution for Occupational Health Externalities
description: The system formalises occupational health externalities using the institutional components of signatures, sentences, models, and satisfaction relations.

Signatures specify actors, hazards, exposures, outcomes, costs, benefits, and control mechanisms; sentences specify claims about causation, burden shifting, hierarchy of controls, and positive spillovers; models interpret how those relations hold in a workplace system; satisfaction tracks whether the translated claims hold under a given parameterisation.

The simulator treats core institutional tensions dynamically: private benefit versus social cost, visible accounts versus hidden burdens, prevention versus deferred harm, precarious flexibility versus protection, compensation versus full internalisation, and negative versus positive externalities.

promptTemplate.title: AI Prompt Generator
promptTemplate.instructions: Add your occupational-health context (e.g., industry, hazards, labour model, affected communities, regulatory setting, hierarchy of controls, compensation regime, positive spillovers). Click “Generate AI Prompt” to produce a prompt for interpreting the current institutional configuration.
promptTemplate.defaultAssumptions: [No additional refinements provided. State reasonable assumptions about the industry, workforce, hazard profile, employment relations, and public systems.]
promptText: You are an expert in occupational health, labour process analysis, health economics, institutional theory, and formal modelling using Goguen institutions.
Write a short analytical report (700–1,000 words) interpreting the current institutional configuration for occupational health externalities.
- The domain concerns workplace harms and benefits that may be externalised or internalised across firms, workers, households, health systems, taxpayers, and communities.
- Signatures include firms, workplaces, production processes, hazards, exposures, outcomes, costs, benefits, accounts, regulations, and control measures.
- Sentences express causal, distributive, regulatory, and preventive claims, including the hierarchy of controls and positive externalities such as training, vaccination, and ergonomic design.
- Models represent concrete sectors or workplaces in which these relations hold with differing degrees of visibility, compensation, enforcement, and prevention.
- Satisfaction concerns whether the model, under the current parameter settings, realises the institution’s claims about externalisation, internalisation, harm propagation, and social benefit.
Please do ALL of the following:
1) Explain how the current parameters shape the model’s likely pattern of negative externalities, including pollution, injury, disease, burnout, family burden, and public-system cost.
2) Interpret the institution in terms of signatures, sentences, models, and satisfaction: which claims are likely to hold strongly, weakly, or fail under this configuration?

3) Discuss the main tensions: private benefit vs social cost, accounting visibility vs material harm, prevention vs deferred liability, precarious employment vs protection, compensation vs full internalisation, and negative vs positive spillovers.

4) Identify the strongest levers for internalising externalities, especially hierarchy-of-control strength, worker voice, regulatory enforcement, compensation coverage, and public reporting/audit capacity.
5) Explain where positive externalities are likely to arise and why they may still be underprovided.
6) Be explicit about uncertainty and what empirical evidence would be needed to validate or refine the institutional model.
Project refinements supplied by the user (incorporate these carefully; if empty, state assumptions you made):

promptUI.refinementsPlaceholder:
Describe your occupational-health externality case here…
Examples:
- Industry and production process
- Main pollutants or psychosocial hazards
- Employment model: direct, outsourced, gig, agency
- Exposed worker groups and nearby communities
- Existing controls in the hierarchy of controls
- Compensation, insurance, and regulatory setting
- Public-system impacts and family burdens
- Training, vaccination, ergonomic, or safety-culture spillovers
- Political or organisational barriers to internalisation

parameters.label: Hazard and pollution intensity
parameters.hint: Higher means production processes generate more toxic, ergonomic, physical, or psychosocial hazard potential at source.

parameters.label: Workload and long-hours pressure
parameters.hint: Higher means greater burnout, fatigue, pace pressure, and work-organisation-related harm.

parameters.label: Precarious / outsourced employment intensity
parameters.hint: Higher shifts risk toward workers, contractors, and households and weakens accountability for prevention and compensation.

parameters.label: Accounting opacity for social cost
parameters.hint: Higher means more of the true occupational and environmental burden remains unrecorded in firm accounts.

parameters.label: Hierarchy-of-controls strength
parameters.hint: Higher means hazards are more likely to be eliminated, substituted, engineered out, or systematically controlled rather than merely tolerated.

parameters.label: Worker voice and collective challenge capacity
parameters.hint: Higher improves reporting, consultation, challenge to unsafe production, and surfacing of hidden harms.

parameters.label: Regulatory enforcement and inspection strength
parameters.hint: Higher increases the chance that harmful externalities are detected, sanctioned, and pushed back onto the originating firm.

parameters.label: Compensation and insurance coverage depth
parameters.hint: Higher means a greater share of work-related harm is compensated, though not necessarily prevented or fully internalised.

parameters.label: Public health / healthcare absorptive capacity
parameters.hint: Higher means health systems and agencies can absorb some burden without immediate breakdown, though the burden may still be socialised.

parameters.label: Community and household exposure linkage
parameters.hint: Higher means workplace harms spill more strongly into surrounding communities, homes, and families.

parameters.label: Audit, reporting, and evidential visibility
parameters.hint: Higher means latent harms, burden shifting, and unequal exposure patterns are more likely to be made legible.

parameters.label: Investment in positive spillovers
parameters.hint: Higher means more training, vaccination, ergonomics, and safety-culture building with benefits that extend beyond the paying firm.

parameters.label: Update rate (ms debounce)
parameters.hint: Higher reduces CPU; lower feels more real-time.

outputs.title: Negative externalisation probability
outputs.subtitle: Probability that the model strongly realises uncompensated occupational harm shifted onto workers, households, public systems, or communities.

outputs.title: Serious worker-harm probability
outputs.subtitle: Probability of high work-related injury, disease, burnout, or chronic strain under the current institutional configuration.

outputs.title: Internalisation and control probability
outputs.subtitle: Probability that regulation, control design, worker voice, audit visibility, and compensation substantially push costs back toward the originating firm and reduce harm.

outputs.title: Positive externality probability
outputs.subtitle: Probability that training, vaccination, ergonomics, or safety-culture investment produce wider benefits beyond the firm that funded them.
